speaker: interviewer
text: April 12th, 1996. Interview with Rachael Rubin, nee Schmutter. This is tape two. Mrs. Rubin, we were talking about when they took you to the police station, and they brought you into the washroom and asked you, or told you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Toilet.

speaker: interviewer
text: --To clean the toilet.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So I was wearing all the time a handkerchief, because the yellow-- the-- the yellow [NON-ENGLISH] what we were wearing. The--

speaker: interviewer
text: The Star of David?

speaker: interviewee
text: Star of David. The star, it was a round [? patch ?]. I was wearing in the back. And my handkerchief always covered-- the point covered the yellow. I was afraid, because it was times when they-- they could shoot when they saw from far a yellow. They shoot right away. They killed people.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was covering. Maybe they won't recognize me. You know, I tried. So I took my handkerchief, I covered my nose and everything, and I cleaned. I cleaned. Then they locked me up when I cleaned the toilet. They locked me up. It went a few hours, and they noticed in there, in the office where I was working, that I'm not there. So they started to look for me. And they came to the police station. And they looked for me, and they started to demand. What? Did she do your-- her work? Why do you keep her? This and this. So then they let me out. The people that came looking for you, they were part of the Judenrat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they released you.

speaker: interviewee
text: They released me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they tell you why you were detained?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know why. There's no answer for this [INAUDIBLE]. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Low. That's it. Very, very low. Very depressed. Very-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] summertime, imagine to go into-- it's not the toilets like today. But they were-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] just impossible. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [SIGHS] And then every-- every day, being in this-- in this office, working there. So every day, we had to give some [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] people to work. Like 20 people, 30 people, to give a list. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They demanded. So we-- we had to give. I mean, the manager. The-- the-- so he gave the list, and-- and he knew that those people wouldn't come back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you think those people were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a few places. We find out [? lately ?] that we had a factory from [? sugar. ?] Far from-- I mean, in the-- in the town, but the end of the town.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after we find out that near the factory they made graves ready, and they shot those people there. People they took for work, they shot. Sometimes the people had to make the graves for themself, and they were shot. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It's unbelievable. At the time, when you were giving the lists and getting the people to go to work--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah?

speaker: interviewer
text: --where did you think they were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: To work. Some even--

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you find out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some even received, like, one wife received a letter from her husband. They made him to write a letter. Said he's-- he's alive, and he's working. And he wasn't around already at that time. But she received a letter. We were thinking maybe, maybe it's true. But they made him to write this letter, because later, we find out that he's not around. He was killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else was happening in the town while you were working for the Judenrat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Once-- when it happened, the slaughter, before [? schul, ?] as I told you, when my mother was killed. So in the morning-- so a young boy from our neighbors came over, and he-- he called me [? Ookie. ?] You know, he didn't-- my name. Like, he-- he banged in the-- on the window in our-- in my room, where I was sleeping. And he said, [? Ookie, ?] they are killing us.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I came out, and I said, [? Pepe, ?] what happened? He said, they are killing us. He ran away from the house. He was with his mother and with his father living in another street. You know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the-- the German with the Ukraine, and came first to their house. And he-- he was able to run away, and he came to my house. And he took us out and said, let's go. They are killing us. And it didn't take long, they were around our house, too. They chased us out. We should go. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So my mother couldn't walk so fast, actually. So then a policeman with a gun gave her a few times on the-- in the back. Hit her. She should walk faster. And they brought us in the street, where one side was men, and the other side, where women were sitting. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And at times, they were selecting, like, the older people, and-- and some older children with small children on wagons. And the younger one were sitting still.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my mother started to-- to push me. And she said, go. Save yourself. Go. They will kill you, too. And so I went into-- I was sitting near a house with a few steps. I went in when the policeman didn't look. You know, he didn't notice. So I went into the house, and I climbed up the attic, and I covered myself with schmatas. And I was lying there for the whole day. Were you with your sister at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My sister was in-- a little bit farther from me. She didn't know that I'm there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went into this house by yourself.

speaker: interviewee
text: By myself. You know. That time, we were so selfish, you know? I never wanted to leave my mother or my sister. My-- my grandmother wasn't alive at the time already. She-- she died.

speaker: interviewer
text: What had happened to her?

speaker: interviewee
text: She died before Pesach. And this was before Shabbos. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So but in this moment, I was so selfish. I didn't think of my mother. And I went into the house, and I hided myself. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the policeman noticed naturally, that I'm missing. So he came over-- he came up on the step ladder. And he was screaming, come down, because you will-- you will be killed! So I figured anyway I'll be killed, this way or the other way. So I didn't want to-- I kept quiet, and that's it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he noticed a few other people there from far. He didn't go up completely, you know. He noticed a few other people, and he took them down, and they were killed. And I was lying there the whole day. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But this slaughter was only one day. You know? In the evening, they announced that now everybody's going to the ghetto. What did you hear of what happened to these people? How did you find out what happened to your mother and to these other people who were hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, my mother was-- I noticed my mother's clothes on the wagon, because she was taken to Korzec. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's like a village. And while she was walking, and my neighbors told me afterwards, said, when she walked past the house there, and she saw the neighbors, she said goodbye to them.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in that time, a lot of people were elderly, younger. But my sister was lucky. Said that a German guy took her out for work. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And And we went to the ghetto after that. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They told us that there's certain streets, that we have to live there. So we didn't have a house anyway. The house was from our best neighbors. They were taking apart the house. There was nothing inside. And I-- I wasn't allowed to-- to live there anymore, anyway. It wasn't in the street where they told us to live. You know? Let's go back for a moment. After these people were marched out, you came down from the attic.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My--

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister came into the house and was screaming, [? Ookie, ?] where are you? Are you still alive? You know, she found me. So after that, we went to the street where the German took us, I mean, to live there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go home? Back to your home at any time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. Maybe I came-- we came to the house, but it was nothing, and scary, you know, to be alone there. Nobody was there from neighbors, from the Jewish neighbors. Nobody was there. So we went, together with all the remainders. What remained. You know? We went to the street where the German told us to stay.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was the same day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in the same day. We couldn't go nowhere. We had to stay there. They were sure that it's going to be another slaughter. We shouldn't be separated, spread out. We should stay together.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were there, but I-- I was trying not to be-- you know, in the-- in the town. I was trying to be outside, because I knew that it's going to come again the same thing. I was afraid to sleep there, you know? How long were you in the ghetto for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Six months. Till Sukkot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's talk a little bit about what life was like for you in the ghetto. Were there fences? How was it? Were there boundaries?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. It was a river. One side a river, I remember. And the other side, it was a market. And the police was all over, you know, all around. So they didn't need fences, and they didn't need-- they knew that we won't run away. There's no way to go. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you get your food from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some people in the ghetto, where they were living before, they prepared themselves, like flour, or sugar, things like that. We shared. You know? And we were allowed, like, during the day to go out for an hour to the market. So we could buy something. Change, like, to give clothes to the Christians, and they gave-- they gave us something else. Food, naturally. That's what the most important thing was the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you treated by the Christians at the market?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's funny. I was wearing a nice handkerchief, you know, with flowers. A very nice one. My sister was a green, and I was a red, I remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the Ukrainian, when they saw me in the market once, she said, you know, why don't you give it to me? You'll be killed anyway. You don't need this. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] For them, we were dead people. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we wanted to-- to cover ourself, to hide ourself, actually, by a Christian. Never they wanted us. The best one, they were afraid of us. They said, we'll be killed, too, if we'll hide you. So we didn't have any place. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] 'Til--

speaker: interviewer
text: What were people talking about when you were in the ghetto? Was there talk of escaping?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some youngsters. It was one guy, Gildenman. He was famous in Israel later. And this guy organized young people, boys mostly, to run away to the partisans. I think [INAUDIBLE] the time he succeeded, because he was in Israel, I heard, and his son was in Israel. A few people were killed by running away from this-- from our town. But a few saved themself being with him. But I, for instance, with my sister, I was trying to be away from Jewish people, to tell you the truth. Because we were afraid. We could hide ourself to put, like, Christian clothes. What they were wearing. I changed the shoes for boots. My nice jacket sweater I changed for a schmata, you know, just to wear. They shouldn't-- they shouldn't recognize us.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you treated by the guards?

speaker: interviewee
text: By the guards? Like garbage. The guards--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't want to be close with them. Close to them. They-- they-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] any little boy could come up and hit me and do whatever he wants with me. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember we had a neighbor with a beard. He was sitting on a bench. You know? And a little boy came, and he was tearing his beard. And the other guy was screaming, and nobody could help him. What would happen-- [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: That what was the treatment from the Ukraine people.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would happen if somebody tried to help?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody was afraid of himself. Nobody could help. I could help just to share my piece of bread, that's all. But physically to help? Impossible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any personal encounters with the guards?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean by personal?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you beaten or were you abused in any way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Once, I remember. He was the principal of the school, and I knew him well. So he was working afterwards in the police. He was a big shot back then.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my grandmother had a cow. You know? She liked her whole milk. To milk the cow. And they took away from us the cow. So. And for us, it was like a terrible thing. We think, why do they take away the cow from us? But they tried all kind of ways to-- to abuse us. Anyway, so but I knew this principal. So I figured I'll go and I'll ask him. Let him tell to give us back the cow because we need the milk. We need. You know what he did? He picked up his hand, and he slapped me in my face. The principal. Go out. That's it. That what was the answer. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And he was teaching us. He was a principal. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then he didn't recognize nobody. So you realized at an-- at an early time that you were basically on your own, and that nobody was going to help you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, definitely. Nobody could help me. That's why I tried with my sister to run away as far as-- as possible, not to be in town.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you plan this escape?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I heard from the gentiles. They said, they are cleaning the graves that were left from the first slaughter. It was left room. They cleaned this. But as soon I heard this, so I right away said in the office, I-- I went over to there where I was working, and I said, I'm going away. I'll try.

speaker: interviewer
text: You told them that you were going to run away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I'm-- I'm trying to go away from here. And because they're-- it's going to be another slaughter. They called it [NON-ENGLISH] The right pronunciation slaughter, and [NON-ENGLISH]. There's going to be another [NON-ENGLISH], so I'm not staying here in town. I'll try.

speaker: interviewee
text: And me with my sister started to walk away from Korcek in the villages, you know? We didn't know exactly the directions. You know? You walked out of the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We walked out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nobody questioned you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we were dressed like, you know, shiksas. So-- and it's during the day. And we walked out before everything happened, you know? Before everything knew that it's going to be another. So we tried to escape.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would have happened to you if you would have been caught?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dead. We would be dead. They would kill us, no question. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: What time of year was it when you left the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was summertime, I remember. Because I remember, my sister was wearing those boots. Till now she suffers with her legs. She was wearing those boots for a week maybe, wandering, walking, and walking. You know? So these feet were swollen, and I couldn't remove them, because they were stuck to the skin. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So we walked till-- I remember the-- the village, [NON-ENGLISH]. The [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH]. Yeah. And there was a Polish lady near-- she lived near the forest. A house near the forest. So I figured an house near the forest is not bad. Maybe she will let us in. We went in, we were-- She was very polite, nice. And-- and she said, you know what? I have some people here in the forest, what I made a place for them. She-- she was digging a big, big hole. And on top of the cover, you know, like from wood, the cover, and dirt and grass on the top, you know, shouldn't notice.

speaker: interviewee
text: Those people are staying there, she said. If they will like, I mean, they will permit to come-- to-- you know, you should join them. I don't mind. And she tells me the names, and I saw it's my-- I know them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- she's now in Israel. [NON-ENGLISH]. A friend of mine. She's older, but we were friends. And another neighbor, and another neighbor, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And [INAUDIBLE] when we came there, she opened the-- this place. And when we came there, you think they were happy? No. They weren't happy. Because it was the place too small. You know? And hardly they could breathe. And to share with food for two more people, it wasn't enough.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they let us in. They let us in, and the next day, it was the German with the Ukraine, surrounded the place, I mean the forest, and they started to shoot. We were inside, and they started to shoot. And they killed a lot of people that time, because a lot of people were in the forest, and they didn't know what happened. They were running from one place to the other. They were killed. And we were inside.

speaker: interviewee
text: But while-- during the day, my sister felt nauseous, and [NON-ENGLISH], the other friend, said, why do I have to stay here and die here? I'll go out. Let them kill me. That's it, let us out. So we opened-- opened the cover, and we let her out, [NON-ENGLISH] and my sister. And we heard the shooting all over, and I was sure that they were killed. But when it stopped after a few hours, maybe the day it lasted, then it stopped. So we went out, because we felt ourselves that we are not able to breathe. So we find them. They were somewhere in a stack of hay. You know, they were covered over, and they were alive. So since then, since this incident, so I told my sister, you know, we won't stay here. I-- I-- I don't like this. Because they don't want us. It's-- it's no use. We are in danger anyway. Let's go.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were dressed differently, as I told you. So we were walking in the same village, and we came to a house, a poor house. And we came in, and they decided to take us in. And naturally, to cover us somewhere in the top of the attic. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then my sister, we tried to cut the boot and to take out her foot, because it was terrible, red and swollen. And I remember, the lady gave her goat milk-- milk from goat. And this was the medication she put on her foot to-- a little bit to cure this. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were staying there for a while. But in meantime, we had to pay them something. So I had-- my mother, actually, had a neighbor, and she-- before the war-- we knew what would happen, so we had like-- my mother had a fur coat. My sister already had a fur coat. So we gave to this lady to cover those things, to my neighbor.

speaker: interviewee
text: And while I was there by this-- in this village, and she demanded from us I mean, to-- to pay her something-- after all, she won't keep us for nothing. So we gave a note that-- that's to-- just to her, a note, she should give the guy the fur coats. You know? We don't need them. So this guy went back to Korcek, and he showed the note, and she gave them away.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was exceptional, intelligent woman, you know. She used to be a teacher years ago. You know. So she gave back those things, and we paid. With those things, we paid for staying in this-- by-- by her. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll go to break now.
